← the Atlas

Distinguish the Active Terminal in a Split

Features· seedling ·proposed ·JUN 21, 2026

When a tile is split into two stacked terminals, nothing marks which pane your keystrokes go to. Paint a quiet accent cue on the pane that currently has focus — driven by the focus signal the split already tracks, no new state.

Contents

  • User-facing description
    • The treatment is a chooser
  • Architecture-level changes
  • Implementation details
    • Open decision — leaning noted

Split a tile and you get two stacked terminals — a main pane on top, a sub-panel below. You click into one, you start typing… and nothing on screen tells you which one is listening. The split already knows — it routes your keystrokes to the right pane and even restores that choice across sessions — but it keeps the secret to itself. This note graduates the Sundry item “Distinguish the active terminal in a split” into a plan: surface that hidden signal as a quiet visual cue.

User-facing description

The cue is a thin teal rail down the inside edge of whichever pane has focus — teal is kolu’s established “you are here” colour. It moves the instant focus moves, so a glance answers “where do my keystrokes land?” without reading anything.

Today vs. proposed — the same split, focus on the sub-panel
argh · claude▢ ✕
$ npm test
PASS · 12 passed
$ ▏
serverlogs
$ vite dev
ready in 240 ms
Today — focus is invisible. Which pane is typing into? You can’t tell.
argh · claude▢ ✕
$ npm test
PASS · 12 passed
$ ▏
serverlogs
$ vite dev
ready in 240 ms
Proposed — the active pane (here, the sub-panel) carries a teal rail.

The cue follows focus. Click the main pane and it jumps up; click back into the split and it drops down — the same motion you already make, now mirrored on screen.

The rail tracks focus as it moves between panes
argh · claude▢ ✕
$ npm test
PASS · 12 passed
$ ▏
serverlogs
$ vite dev
ready in 240 ms
Main pane focused — rail on top.
argh · claude▢ ✕
$ npm test
PASS · 12 passed
$ ▏
serverlogs
$ vite dev
ready in 240 ms
Sub-panel focused — rail on the bottom.

It shows only inside the tile you’re working in and only while the split is open — a collapsed split has just one terminal, so there’s nothing to distinguish. The active sub-tab already has its own highlight in the tab bar; this is the missing, coarser signal: main vs. sub.

The treatment is a chooser

Three quiet, static languages — pick one during plan review. All reuse the same teal accent and add no motion, so prefers-reduced-motion needs no special case.

Same state (main focused) in three visual languages
argh · claude▢ ✕
$ npm test
PASS · 12 passed
$ ▏
serverlogs
$ vite dev
ready in 240 ms
A · Edge rail recommended
A 2px bar on the active pane’s inside edge. Smallest footprint; never touches the terminal text.
argh · claude▢ ✕
$ npm test
PASS · 12 passed
$ ▏
serverlogs
$ vite dev
ready in 240 ms
B · Inset ring
A faint accent ring around the whole active pane. Louder; encloses the pane but boxes in the xterm.
argh · claude▢ ✕
$ npm test
PASS · 12 passed
$ ▏
serverlogs
$ vite dev
ready in 240 ms
C · Recede the other
Dim the inactive pane instead. Strong “pop,” but it greys out live output and overlaps the canvas “parked tile” dim.

Why A is the lead

The rail is the least intrusive — it never dims live terminal output (rules out C, which also collides with the canvas’s existing parked-tile dim) and never draws a box around the xterm grid (B). It’s a single edge in the one colour kolu already means by “focus.”

Architecture-level changes

There is almost nothing structural to change — and that’s the point. The split already tracks which pane has focus: focusTarget: "main" | "sub" lives in the per-tile useSubPanel store, is read on every keystroke to route input, and is even persisted for session restore. The work is to render that existing signal, not to invent a new one.

  • Reuse the source of truth, don’t fork it. The cue reads focusTarget — the same field that routes keystrokes (packages/client/src/terminal/useSubPanel.ts). A parallel “which pane looks active” flag, or a CSS :focus-within shortcut that re-derives the same fact a second way, would be two sources that can disagree. One signal, two consumers (input routing + the cue).
  • A presentational leaf, not electricity. This hides no hard volatility (transport, reconnect, GPU-context loss) — it’s a bounded bit of styling inside the terminal module. It stays in TerminalContent + the global stylesheet; it earns no @kolu/* package.
  • No knob, no fallback. The cue is always on whenever a split is open and focused — no preference toggle (an override is a defect, not a feature) and no degraded path. It either reflects focusTarget or, if that signal were ever absent, the split itself would already be broken upstream.
  • Paint over, never reflow. The cue is a CSS overlay drawn above the pane, exactly like the canvas tile-aura (packages/client/src/canvas/CanvasTile.tsx) — pointer-events: none, no border or padding that changes the pane’s box. That matters: any size change to a pane forces xterm to refit its grid and resize the PTY. The cue must cost zero layout.

Implementation details

Small and contained — one component gains a derived flag, the stylesheet gains a rule, and the Sundry row graduates to this note.

  1. Mark the active pane in packages/client/src/terminal/TerminalContent.tsx. It already computes shouldFocusMain() / shouldFocusSub() from props.focused, isExpanded(), and focusTarget() — the cue keys off the same conditions. Tag each of the two Resizable.Panels with a data-pane-active marker (the focused pane only), so the live pane is addressable from CSS.
  2. Paint the rail in packages/client/src/index.css — a data-pane-active rule using --color-accent, drawn as a non-layout overlay (mirroring the .tile-aura block already in that file). Static; no prefers-reduced-motion branch needed.
  3. Graduate the Sundry item — remove the row from docs/atlas/src/content/atlas/sundry.mdx (per its own “Graduating an item” rule) and leave a one-line pointer here.
  4. Docs + changelog — this note is the design of record; add an Added line to the changelog. No README/marketing surface lists split-pane focus behaviour, so the doc-sync is this note plus the changelog.

Test (feature, so test-first): an e2e scenario that opens a split, focuses the main pane then the sub-panel, and asserts data-pane-active lands on the right pane each time — reusing the existing split/sub-terminal steps (packages/tests/step_definitions/sub_terminal_steps.ts).

Risks, and why they’re small

  • xterm refit on a layout change — avoided by construction: the cue is an overlay, not a border, so no pane box changes size.
  • Two “active” signals competing — the cue is gated to the focused tile, so it never lights a pane in a tile you’re not in; the canvas’s active-tile outline and this inner rail live on different surfaces and can’t be confused.
  • Mobile — TerminalContent is shared by the canvas and the mobile tile view, so the cue appears in both with no extra work; evidence will cover the mobile layout.

Open decision — leaning noted

  • Scope: focused tile only (leaning) vs. every open split. Showing it only in the tile you’re in keeps the canvas calm and removes any “is teal = active tile?” ambiguity. Showing it everywhere would let you read each split’s pending pane at a glance, but adds visual noise. Lean: focused tile only; revisit if the always-on version proves more useful in practice.

Status: proposed — plan of record for graduating the Sundry item. The PR link lands here (<PrLink />) once the change ships.

Referenced by
